title: Understand: Why have a civil dialogue?
description: Check your understanding of the importance of having a civic dialogue in this set of free practice questions.
topic_path: Civil Dialogue, The Constitution, and Constitutional Interpretation
Understand: Why have a civil dialogue?

assessment_items.question_content:
**When having a constitutional conversation with someone, you should try to do which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Make sure that you are asking constitutional questions, not political questions.
Always ask only “yes or no” questions.
Try to incorporate scholarly work into your answers.
Only use primary sources as evidence to support your argument.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which action should you take when having a constitutional conversation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Use general ideas that are only loosely connected to the question.
Talk about scholarly ideas as if they are your own to strengthen your argument.
Provide explanations for what the information you are citing means.
Present evidence on its own without adding any additional commentary.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is true about the nature of discussions between justices of the Supreme Court?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The justices occasionally raise their voices at each other to show their passion for a subject.
The justices are civil, polite, and professional, even if they strongly disagree.
The justices are more likely to engage in discussion with politically-aligned justices.
The justices will only speak in order of seniority to ensure the most opinions are heard.

assessment_items.question_content:
Justice Stephen Breyer talked about how the Supreme Court settles arguments and how their method can work for you, too. 

**What was Justice Breyer’s advice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Only raise your voice if it will help you win.
Always stick to your argument.
Focus only on what you are saying.
Stay calm and listen to others.

assessment_items.question_content:
Justice Breyer suggests the following tip for having a conversation with a group of people: “Don’t speak twice until everyone has spoken once.” 

**To whom should this suggestion apply?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Only justices of the Supreme Court
Only groups with many people
Only members of Congress
Only members of your school’s debate team

assessment_items.question_content:
**In the Supreme Court case of *Whitney v. California* (1927), which justice emphasized the importance of civil dialogue?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: John Marshall
Earl Warren
Thurgood Marshall
Louis Brandeis

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which groups should have conversations about the United States Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Supreme Court justices
Foreign ambassadors to the United States
Ordinary citizens, regardless of their profession or background
Members of national, state, and local governments

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to Alexander Hamilton, what is the purpose of the Federalist Papers?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To lay out arguments for the ratification of the Constitution
To encourage colonists to rebel against the British crown
To argue for the addition of a Bill of Rights to the Constitution
To discourage the secession of the southern states

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to Jeffrey Rosen, which of these is a requirement of an open democracy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Expressing your opinion loudly
Collaborating with others who hold similar opinions
Listening to other points of view
Contacting judges to tell them your opinions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which founder may have inspired this quote from Justice Louis Brandeis: "They believed that freedom to think as you will and to speak as you think"?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: George Washington
Thomas Jefferson
James Madison
Benjamin Franklin

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the best definition of civil dialogue?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Heated debate about how the government should run and what policies should be implemented
Academic discussions in which people discuss topics only if they have expert knowledge to contribute
Polite conversation in which no one talks about controversial political topics that could upset someone
Respectful conversation in which the goal is to listen to other points of view and share different perspectives

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is one message Thomas Jefferson tried to convey to the American people in his first inaugural address?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To support the end of political parties in American politics, as they divided the American people
To warn against the influence of foreign powers and governments on American politics
To be civil with those who held different political beliefs and unite as American people
To encourage people to consider the proposal for the new Constitution carefully rather than reject it outright
